preFix.title: Tension and twist ratios explained
preFix.subtitle: How drive ratio, whorl size, and twist per inch work together on the spinning wheel
preFix.excerpt: The twist in a handspun yarn is controlled by the spinning wheel's drive ratio, set by the size of the drive wheel relative to the whorl on the flyer shaft. Understanding how drive ratio relates to twist per inch allows a spinner to plan yarn deliberately rather than by feel alone.
preFix.sourceNotes: Wheel mechanics and drive ratio calculation are consistent across wheel manufacturers' documentation and UK spinning guild teaching. Mabel Ross's 'The Essentials of Yarn Design' is the primary reference for the twist-per-inch approach to yarn planning.

content.text: The amount of twist in a handspun yarn determines much of its character: a fine lace yarn needs high twist to hold together; a bulky woollen yarn needs low twist to stay soft and airy. The spinning wheel controls twist through its
, the relationship between the size of the main drive wheel and the size of the
on the flyer shaft. A spinner who understands this relationship can plan a yarn deliberately rather than adjusting by feel alone.

The drive ratio is the diameter of the main drive wheel divided by the diameter of the whorl on the flyer shaft. A wheel with a 50 cm drive wheel and a 5 cm whorl has a drive ratio of 10:1. Each complete rotation of the drive wheel causes the flyer to rotate ten times, inserting ten turns of twist into the forming yarn. A 6 cm whorl on the same wheel gives a ratio of approximately 8:1, inserting fewer twists per treadle stroke. Most wheels list their available ratios in the manufacturer's documentation; if yours does not, measure the drive wheel and each whorl with a ruler and calculate the ratio directly.

(TPI) in a finished yarn is the objective measure of how much twist was inserted during spinning. To measure TPI: cut a 5 cm length of yarn, hold one end still, and untwist the other end while counting the rotations until all the plies or fibres lie parallel. Divide the rotation count by the length in inches (for a 5 cm sample, divide by approximately 2). For a singles yarn, hold one end and count rotations until individual fibres lie straight. Typical TPI ranges by weight: lace-weight 8 to 12 TPI, DK 5 to 8 TPI, bulky 2 to 4 TPI. These are starting points rather than fixed rules; fibre type and intended use alter the preferred range.

As a working rule: use a low drive ratio (4:1 to 6:1) for bulky and chunky yarns; a medium ratio (7:1 to 9:1) for DK and aran weights; a high ratio (10:1 to 16:1) for lace-weight and thread. Fibre type modifies this: short-staple fibres such as merino or Corriedale need more twist than long-staple lustre breeds at the same yarn weight, because shorter fibres need more turns to lock together. A long-staple fibre like Wensleydale can produce a balanced DK single at a lower ratio than Corriedale of the same diameter. The longer fibres interlock with fewer turns, so less twist is needed.
Changing the ratio on your wheel

To change the drive ratio, move the drive band from the current whorl groove to a different groove on the flyer shaft. A smaller groove increases the ratio; a larger groove decreases it. On castle-style wheels the whorl is usually on the flyer shaft and bobbins are interchangeable. On some wheels the bobbin itself carries the whorl, in which case you switch bobbins rather than grooves. After changing the ratio, adjust the drive band tension so it has the same light, consistent pressure as before, then run the wheel briefly by hand to confirm the flyer is turning freely before you begin spinning.
